coredata.dc:title: Growth mechanism of rGO/CDs by electrospun calcination process: Structure and application
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Vinit
Anit Kaur, Gun
Gupta, Neeraj
Shandilya, Mamta
coredata.dc:description: A facile, efficient and novel strategy was developed for the preparation of reduced Graphene Oxide/Carbon Dots (rGO/CDs) by electrospinning and annealing of an organic polymer. In the present investigation, rGO/CDs was prepared from poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropylene) [P(VDF-HFP] based nanofiber fabricated via electrospinning technique followed by calcination. Formation of rGO/CDs was confirmed with the help of X-Ray diffraction (XRD). FTIR and XPS analyses indicate the presence of C and O in nanostructure, which demonstrates that this method can efficiently convert calcined polymeric nanofiber into rGO/CDs. FE-SEM micrographs reveal the morphological changes of the nanofiber membrane during the calcination process. The material shows excellent property to detect dopamine (DA). In the case of CV, a linear relationship for the DA concentration is observed in the range of 0.5 µM to 20 µM and, linear regression equation is y = 8.32667x + 0.0524 through correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.99174, with a detection limit of 1.41 µM. All the electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.2 as a supporting electrolyte. Therefore, the rGO/CDs/GCE electrode can be effectively applied to the quantitative analysis of DA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
rGO/CDs
Nanofiber
Electrospinning
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of concentrations of rGO/CDs for electrode modification
Voltammetric behaviours of DA at rGO/CDs/GCE surface
Effect of different concentrations of DA at rGO/CDs/GCE by cyclic voltammetry
Measurement of limit of detection (LOD) by linear relationship for DA concentration

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation facility (SAIF), Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing characterisation facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of PVDF-HFP nanofiber membrane (a) before calcination and after calcination, (b) peak deconvolution of XRD spectrum.
FTIR spectrum of PVDF-HFP nanofiber membrane (a) before calcination and (b) after calcination.
(a) C 1s XPS spectrum of rGO/CDs and (b) O 1s XPS spectrum of rGO/CDs.
(a) FE-SEM micrograph of polymeric nanofiber membrane before calcination process and (b) Histogram representing fiber diameter of the polymeric nanofiber membrane.
FE-SEM images of (a) sheet formation, (b), (c) and (d) tube formation of the nanofiber membrane after calcination process at different resolutions.
TEM images of synthesized rGO/CDs at (a) low magnification (100 nm), (b), (c)] high magnification and (d) Histogram representing diameter distribution of CDs on the surface of rGO.

(a) Variation of oxidation peak current of DA obtained by modification of GCE by different concentrations of rGO/CDs suspension. (b) Cyclic voltammogram of bare/GCE …….. that was recorded in phosphate buffer solution.
Cyclic voltammograms of 1 mM DA at bare GCE and rGO/CDs/GCE with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1.
Cyclic voltammograms of different concentration of DA at rGO/CDs/GCE with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1.
Effect of concentration of DA on the oxidation peak current by using cyclic voltammetry.
Comparison of results for the determination of DA using different electrodes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPV – differential pulse voltammetry; LSV – linear sweep voltammetry.

head.ce:title: Growth mechanism of rGO/CDs by electrospun calcination process: Structure and application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Bajhol, Solan, H.P 173229, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Sciences, Central University of Dharamshala (H.P.), TAB Shahpur, Kangra 176206, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, and versatile approach was developed for the synthesis of reduced graphene oxide/ Carbon Dots (rGO/CDs) via electrospinning method. This novel approach gives a new horizon to prepare rGO/CDs with a facile and efficient technique. The synthesized rGO was characterized by using XRD, FTIR, FE-SEM, TEM and XPS. In addition, the electrochemical behavior of dopamine at the surface of rGO/CDs modified GCE in dopamine has also been demonstrated. The modified GCE shows excellent electrochemical activity for the rapid response, selectivity and sensitivity towards the determination of DA.

A facile, efficient and novel strategy was developed for the preparation of reduced Graphene Oxide/Carbon Dots (rGO/CDs) by electrospinning and annealing of an organic polymer. In the present investigation, rGO/CDs was prepared from poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropylene) [P(VDF-HFP] based nanofiber fabricated via electrospinning technique followed by calcination. Formation of rGO/CDs was confirmed with the help of X-Ray diffraction (XRD). FTIR and XPS analyses indicate the presence of C and O in nanostructure, which demonstrates that this method can efficiently convert calcined polymeric nanofiber into rGO/CDs. FE-SEM micrographs reveal the morphological changes of the nanofiber membrane during the calcination process. The material shows excellent property to detect dopamine (DA). In the case of CV, a linear relationship for the DA concentration is observed in the range of 0.5 µM to 20 µM and, linear regression equation is y = 8.32667x + 0.0524 through correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.99174, with a detection limit of 1.41 µM. All the electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.2 as a supporting electrolyte. Therefore, the rGO/CDs/GCE electrode can be effectively applied to the quantitative analysis of DA.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials have been used for a long time in electrochemical detection due to their ability to act as direct catalysts or modifiers. They can suit most of the advantageous properties such as high strength, tremendous resistance; superior electrical conductivity and thermal conduction that make them suitable for various chemical and biological applications [1]. However, the use of carbon materials in electrochemical detection will depend on the subsequent control of the chemistry of the carbon surface which can be exploited in the design of truly unique catalysts for electrode modification [2,3]. Among various carbon materials, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has garnered significant attention in the scientific community and been intensively exploited as a functional material for high performance electrochemical sensors due to its peculiar electronic properties, robust transport properties and excellent thermal properties [4]. Till now, numerous studies for the preparation of rGO have been reported [5–8]. Broadly speaking, the chemical synthesis followed for the preparation of rGO involves three main steps i.e. oxidation, exfoliation, and reduction [9]. The first step involves the oxidation of graphite into graphite oxide, followed by an exfoliation of graphite oxide into graphene oxide (GO) and finally the last step where GO undergoes reduction process to form reduced graphene oxide (rGO). However, the steps followed for its preparation are generally complex, expensive, involve slow oxidation process and not industrially scalable [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs) are another well-known carbon nanomaterials with zero-dimension and superior properties like low toxicity, small dimension and remarkable conductivity have grabbed extensive attention in the scientific community. The small size of CDs on the surface of rGO will increase the active sites for the corresponding reaction [12]. Moreover, the hybrid formation of rGO/CDs will be more smooth and uniform since the lattice parameters of both carbon materials are similar in nature. However, the synthesis process involved in the formation of the nanoscale hetero structure between rGO and CDs involves multi-step methods. Hence, there is a need to develop a facile one-step route for the preparation of rGO/CDs.

Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitters in the central nervous, hormonal and renal systems [13]. DA is mainly present in blood serum and low levels of DA concentration are responsible for several diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease [14], attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) [15], restless legs syndrome (RLS) [16] as well as schizophrenia [17]. DA is a type of catecholamine that can be usually detected by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [18] high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [19] and chemiluminescence [20]. However, these analytical methods have various limitations, such as long running time, low-sensitivity and require expensive apparatus as well as a large amount of solvent. It can be easily detected by electrochemical method because of its fast response, low cost, high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and conductivity. In the electrochemical sensing technique, direct detection of DA is very difficult because of its poor response. Therefore, the chemical modification of electrode surfaces is required to increase the selectivity and sensitivity of electrochemical sensors [21,22]. In current years, several modifiers materials with excellent conductivity, high stability, good catalytic activity such as carbon materials [23], polymers [24], metal oxide and nanoparticles [25] have been used for the modification of bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surfaces.

In order to combat the aforementioned problems, we propose a novel, and versatile approach for the synthesis of rGO/CDs via electrospinning method. This novel approach gives a new horizon to prepare rGO/CDs with a facile and efficient technique. Furthermore, the electrochemical behavior of dopamine on the surface of rGO/CDs modified GCE in dopamine has also been demonstrated. The modified GCE shows excellent electrochemical activity for the rapid response, selectivity and sensitivity towards the determination of DA.

Dopamine (98%) was purchased from the Himedia. The potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate monobasic (KH2PO4) and di-hydrogen orthophosphate dibasic (K2HPO4) both were purchased from CDH. Acetone (99.5%) was purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd and 0.05 µM polishing alumina slurry were purchased from ALS. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Loba ChemiePvt. Ltd and Poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropylene) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Pure distilled water was used for the preparation of buffer and DA solution.

The schematic representation of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 . A solution of N, N Dimethylformamide/ Acetone (3:2 ratio) was used to disperse Poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropylene) [P(VDF-HFP)]; (Mw = 4,75,000 g/mole) pellets (10 wt% solution) at 50 °C for 24 h until the desired viscosity was achieved. The as-prepared solution was filled into the plastic syringe of 5 mL and the spinneret was clipped with the positive terminal of the high voltage power supply, while clipping the other wire to the aluminium foil placed on the collector plate. The solution was fed at the rate of 0.5 mL/h. The distance between the collector and the tip of the spinneret was 15 cm, and the applied voltage was set at 16 kV. The electric field overcomes the surface tension of polymer solution, spilling out the solution of the spinneret. As a result, the polymer solution converted into a plurality of threads and was collected onto the collector. After the completion of electrospinning process, the electrospun nanofiber membrane was peeled off the aluminium foil and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h at a rate of 2 °C/min.

The electrospun nanofiber membrane was characterized by XRD, FTIR, FE-SEM and TEM. The structural properties were studied by XRD pattern using PW3040 Philips X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) and FTIR spectra by using Cary630, Agilent Technologies FTIR spectrophotometer in the range of 4000–650 cm−1. The lattice morphology and structure of these samples were characterized by FE-SEM (Hitachi SU8010 series).All the electrochemical experiments were performed with Bio-logic SP 200 using a standard three electrode system: rGO/CDs/GCE as a working electrode; Ag/AgCl was used as reference electrode and platinum wire was employed as a counter electrode. All the electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M Phosphate Buffer Solution (PBS) of pH 7.2 as a supporting electrolyte. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) was used for the detection of DA through rGO/CDs/GCE.

The phase of the nanofiber membrane before and after the calcination process were studied with the help of XRD measured in the range of 2θ = 10° to 80° as shown in Fig. 2 . XRD patterns exhibit the significant characteristic diffraction peak appearing at 2θ = 20.6 (200/110) and 2θ = 17.8 (100) corresponding to the β-phase and α-phase, respectively, without any impurities or secondary phase as shown in Fig. 2.(a). Interestingly, after the calcination process, a gradual change in the patterns is discerned. The XRD spectrum is merged into a single broad diffraction peak centering at a relatively higher angle i.e. 2θ = 23.9 (002) demonstrating theπ-conjugated graphitic framework of rGO. In addition, the weaker (100) peak confirms the existence of carbon phases [26] and thus, indicates the formation of rGO/CDs hybrid. This could be ascribed to the removal of functional groups of P(VDF-HFP) during the calcination process. The d-spacing of the most intense peak was calculated using Bragg’s equation
(1) nλ = 2dsinθ
where n is an integer, λ is the wavelength of the radiation for Cu Kα,
θ
is the angle between the incident and reflected rays. The d-spacing calculated from the major peak of P(VDF-HFP) before calcination (200) plane comes out to be 1.59 nm whereas the calcination process changes the chain re-organization and it is decreased to 1.37 nm which can be attributed to the removal of some functional groups and moisture containing oxygen and hydrogen [27]. Fig. 2.(b) shows the deconvolution of XRD peaks for both samples using Gaussian fit.

FTIR measurements result in spectrum showing bands originating from the vibrations of individual bonds or groups within the prepared sample. Fig. 3 . shows the FTIR spectra of PVDF-HFP nanofiber membrane after and before calcination between 4000 and 650 cm−1. All corresponding peaks in Fig. 3.(b) have relatively smaller intensities as compared to Fig. 3.(a). This significant reduction of the intensities of all the oxygen containing moieties indicates the conversion of polymer PVDF-HFP-based nanofiber membrane into rGO/CDs. It can be clearly observed that the prepared PVDF-HFP nanofiber membrane consists of many absorption peaks that correspond to various oxygen containing functional groups; C-O stretching (1072 cm−1), and C-O-C stretching (1180 cm−1) [33]. After the calcination process, the intensities of all the aforementioned peaks have merged. The peaks corresponding to 1706 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 are ascribed to the skeletal vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in CO group and carboxylic CC group, respectively [28]. The comparison of both spectra indicates that calcination process is effective to remove the oxygen-containing functional groups from the nanofiber membrane and generate a surface rich in sp2 hybridized carbon.

The elemental composition of the as prepared rGO/CDs was determined with the help of XPS. The survey spectrum indicates the presence of C and O as shown in Fig. 4 .The result for C 1s spectrum of rGO/CDs shows three prominent peaks centered at binding energy values of 284.9, 286.5 and 288.7 eV corresponding to CC, CO, and CO, respectively as shown in Fig. 5 .(a). Additionally, O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at binding energy values of 533.4 eV and 531.9 eV corresponding to C-O and CO, respectively as shown in Fig. 5.(b). Moreover, the intense and sharp peak centered at a binding energy of 284.9 eV can be attributed to carbon with sp2 hybridization. The contribution of this peak to the total C 1s signal was found to be 85.45%, whereas for O 1s signal, the contribution was found to be 14.55%. This clearly indicates that the surface is rich in sp2 C content with oxygen containing groups.

Fig. 6 . provides a glimpse into the possible orientations and changes in the morphology of the nanofiber membrane before and after the calcination process at different resolutions. Fig. 6.(a) demonstrates the morphology of the nanofiber before calcination process. The surface of randomly oriented polymeric nanofibers was smooth and free from any voids or beads with an average diameter of ~416 ± 10 nm as shown in the histogram of fiber diameter [Fig. 6.(b)]. The calcination process is expected to enhance the dimensional integrity, mechanical properties of the electrospun nanofiber and fuse the fibers together. After the calcination process, the nanofibers change their morphologies into sheet (with a flat surface) or tube like structures. In Fig. 7 .(a), it can be clearly seen that the sheets thus formed have rolled to form a tube-like structure with an average length of 12.28 μm. and diameter of about 900 nm as shown in Fig. 7.(b). The tubes formed were not less than 900 nm range and further reduction in the size of tubes were not observed. The layers are rolled into a tube-like structure, which is attributed to the rolling and fusing of the corner edges of the flat surface during the annealing process. Closer examination of the tube reveals a clearer picture where few hollow spaces at equal intervals within the tube structure in the direction of length of the nanofiber is easily discernible as shown in Fig. 7.(c). These hollow spaces facilitate for higher conduction or charge transport within the material which may provide enhanced conductivity. It is also noteworthy that the corners of the tubes are closed which suggests the formation of hybrid structure of carbon tubes (900 nm diameter) as shown in Fig. 7.(d).

Fig. 8 .[(a), (b)] shows the low and high magnification of TEM images of the synthesized sample, respectively, presenting the distribution of a few dots (red circles) with their size on thenanometre scale. These small spherical shaped dark dots most probably are the carbon dots which are dominantly dispersed on the homogenous grey surface of rGO. The electrospinning technique successfully promotes the uniform in situ decoration of CDs on the rGO framework. The existence of oxygen containing defect sites on the surface of rGO provides an opportunity to uniformly adhere CDs to rGO surface. Furthermore, the CDs act as spacers or intercalators that effectively hinder the restacking of nanosheets, leading to greater surface area as well as pore volume. These enhanced properties help the penetration of ions internally, leading to enhanced electrochemical performance of the synthesized nanocomposite (rGO/ CDs). Careful examination of the CDs under higher magnification as shown in Fig. 8.(c) revealed that their size lie within the range of 2.5–3 nm as shown in the histogram of sample diameter presented in Fig. 8.(d). These results are also in good agreement with the XRD results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of concentrations of rGO/CDs for electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: Seven different suspensions of rGO/CDs were prepared with 1, 2, 6, 8, 10, and12 mg amount in 2 mL acetone followed by sonication for 30 min. The conditions were optimized to achieve better sensitivity and selectivity for oxidation peak current of DA. The redox peak current was significantly influenced by the amount of rGO/CDs casted on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE). When 10 µL suspension of different concentrations was spread on the surface of the GCE, cyclic voltammograms showed that oxidation peak current of DA increases with increase in the rGO/CDs concentration. DA peak current reaches a maximum value when the concentration of rGO/CDs was 6 mg in 2 mL acetone and further an increase in the concentration of rGO/CDs results in a decrease of the oxidation peak current [Fig. 9 .(a)]. The decrease in current is most probably due to the increase in the thickness of the rGO/CDs coating on the electrode surface, which further introduces more intervening layers between the electrode surface and DA leading to decrease in the oxidation peak current. The cyclic voltammograms for the electrochemical response of bare/GCE in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH = 7.2) as a supporting electrolyte with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 is shown in Fig. 9.(b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric behaviours of DA at rGO/CDs/GCE surface
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of DA (1 mM) was studied at a bare and rGO/CDs/GCE surface that was modified by using 6 mg rGO/CDs in 2 mL acetone. Dopamine gives a pair of peaks in both rGO/CDs and bare/GCE in the potential range of −0.1 to 0.1 V. There was an increase in peak current when the GCE was modified with rGO (curve 2) in comparison to bare/GCE(curve 1), while the peak potential difference is decreased. The electrochemical oxidation of DA in aqueous solution goes through one step according mechanism presented in Scheme 1 . In the second scan (Fig. 10 ., curve 2) dopamine is oxidized to dopaquinone (peak a1), and next, it is reduced in part to DA (peak b1). The peak potentials are Epa = 0.042 V, and Epc = 0.021 V at the bare/GCE, while Epa = 0.118 V, and Epc = 0.057 V at the rGO/GCE indicating that the modified electrode can efficiently catalyze the oxidation process, and electron transfer rate of DA in the rGO modified surface was much faster (Fig. 10.). These are probably due to the electrocatalytic oxidation of the pretreated GCE to dopamine. The electrocatalytic oxidation mechanism of dopamine at the modified electrode is shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different concentrations of DA at rGO/CDs/GCE by cyclic voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of different solutions of DA (0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 µM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.2) was also determined at a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1 by cyclic voltammetry by using 6 mg rGO/CDs in 2 mL acetone as a coating material for electrode. . The capability of the modified rGO/CDs/GCE electrode was determined for the quantification of DA. Cyclic voltammograms of DA at the bare and the modified electrode are shown in Fig. 11 .

It can be observed that almost small peak current response of DA was observed at the bare electrode, while the peak current response of DA sharply increases by increasing the concentration at the rGO/CDs/GCE electrode, indicating that the rGO/CDs can significantly catalyze the DA oxidation process and that the electron transfer rate of DA in the rGO/CDs/GCE is much faster. This may be attributed to the special chemical and nano-mesh structure of rGO/CDs that is rich in surface oxygenated groups and electron conducting sp2 hybrid carbon. The polar oxygenated groups may help in surface attachment of DA molecules and sp2 C rich surface may assist in electron transfer. All these unique physical and chemical properties significantly improve the DA reactivity at the rGO/CDs/GCE and significantly increase the response of signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of limit of detection (LOD) by linear relationship for DA concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
The LOD of the DA was calculated from the plot of concentration vs anodic peak current (Ipa). The dependence of the anodic peak current on the concentration of DA for CV is shown in Fig. 12 . In the case of CV, a linear relationship for the dopamine concentration is observed in the range of 0.5 µM to 20 µM and, linear regression equation is y = 8.32667x + 0.0524with a correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.99174 as shown in Fig. 12. The limit of LOD of DA determination was calculated from the following relationship given below:
(2) LOD = 3 s/m
where s is the standard deviation of the anodic peak current of the blank signal and m is the slope of the calibration plot. The LOD of DA was found to be 1.41 µM. Thus, the prepared electrode allows for the sensitive determination of DA in a moderately large concentration range. The comparison of the results for the determination of DA by using carbon material modified electrodes is summarized in Table 1 . For the method based on the CV, the linear peak current, concentration range, and detection limit were comparable or similar in comparison to the electrochemical procedures of DA detection at the electrodes modified with carbon material presented in the literature. The rGO/CDs/GCE shows the comparable analytical performance of DA determination to electrodes modified with other carbon nanomaterials and metallic nanoparticles. These results indicate that the rGO/CDs can be a good electrode material to develop electro-analytical methods to determine DA in real or commercial samples.

In conclusion, nanoscale heterostructure of rGO/CDs was successfully synthesized via a simple, cost-effective and versatile approach. The XRD analysis clearly corroborates the formation of rGO/CDs after the nanofiber membrane was calcined. The FTIR spectrum shows the removal of certain oxygen containing moieties. FE-SEM micrographs demonstrate the shape of the formed rGO/CDs akin to nanotubes, which is the main reason behind its high electrical conductivity. A sensitive and discriminating electrochemical sensor with rGO/CDs/GCE was established for the determination of DA. The rGO/CDs modified GCE electrode has shown considerably superior peak current towards the oxidation of DA. The rGO/CDs/GCE based electrochemical sensor has also displayed superb electrocatalytic activity and electron transfer rate towards the dopamine oxidation. Moreover, the sensor electrode possesses good sensitivity, conductivity, selectivity and low detection limit in the simultaneous detection of DA. These outcomes reveal that rGO/CDs/GCE could be a potential material for electrochemical and biosensor applications. All the results indicate that the rGO/CDs based materials can be synthesized by electrospinning and annealing technique that generates a good electrode material for electro-analytical determination of DA in real or commercial samples.

Vinit Sharma: Validation, Writing - original draft. Gun Anit Kaur: Software, Visualization, Formal analysis. Neeraj Gupta: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration. Mamta Shandilya: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation facility (SAIF), Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing characterisation facilities.

Y. Fan, H.-T. Lu, J.-H. Liu, C.-P. Yang, Q.-S. Jing, Y.-X. Zhang, X.-K. Yang, K.-J. Huang, Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces, 83 (2011) 78-82.
Z. Zhuang, J. Li, R. Xu, D. Xiao, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci, 6 (2011) 2149-2161.
J.-B. Raoof, R. Ojani, S. Rashid-Nadimi, Electrochimica Acta, 50 (2005) 4694-4698.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modification of structural and magnetic properties of Co0. 5Ni0. 5Fe2O4 nanoparticles embedded Polyvinylidene Fluoride nanofiber membrane via electrospinning method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kaur GA, et al., Modification of structural and magnetic properties of Co0. 5Ni0. 5Fe2O4 nanoparticles embedded Polyvinylidene Fluoride nanofiber membrane via electrospinning method, Nano-Structures & Nano-Objects 22 (2020), doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoso.2020.100428.
